coredata.dc:title: The electrochemical behaviors and kinetics of AuNPs/N, S-GQDs composite electrode: A novel label-free amplified BPA aptasensor with extreme sensitivity and selectivity
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Jun
Li, Yang
Xie, Ming
Yang, Qing
Liu, Tongyi
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptasensor
Nitrogen-sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical kinetics
Au nanoparticles
Bisphenol A
Detection mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Explore the physical and chemical properties of N, S-GQDs
Reaction kinetic mechanism and parameter calculation
The sensitivity of AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/aptamer sensor
Selectivity and stability of the sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The sensing mechanism of the AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/aptamer probe.

(A) SEM and (B) TEM images with different magnifications of the AuNPs. (C) TEM size distribution of the AuNPs (a–g: 10, 20, 30, 50, 80, 100, >100 nm). (D) FTIR spectrum of N, S-GQDs. (E) UV absorption spectrum of N, S-GQDs. Top-right inset are photographs of N, S-GQDs solution under (a) visible and (b) UV light of 365 nm. (F) Fluorescence spectra of the N, S-GQDs excited at different excitation wavelengths and (G) XRD pattern of N, S-GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) Simulation XRD pattern of N, S-GQDs and (B) density of states of N, S-GQDs.

(A) The CV spectra of aptamer probe at different scan rate ranges. (B) The linear relationships between (a) oxidation peak and (b) reduction peak current and square root of scanning speed. (C) The linear relationship between oxidation peak potential and scan rate logarithm. (D) The linear fit curves of charge and square root of time for (a) the aptamer sensor and (b) the bare electrode. Characterization of different modification steps of the sensing surface by (E) CV in 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 M KCl at a scan rate of 50 mV·s−1, and (F) EIS in 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 M KCl solution at test frequency range of 0.1–100,000 Hz for (a) AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/Aptamer/GCE (b) N, S-GQDs/GCE (c) AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/GCE and (d) Bare GCE.

(A) DPV of the AuNPs/N, S-GQDs aptamer sensor in 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 M KCl, after incubation with different concentration of BPA in Tris-HCl buffer solution. Concentrations range (a–i): 0 μM, 0.01 μM, 0.1 μM, 0.5 μM, 1 μM, 2 μM, 5 μM, 10 μM and 100 μM. (B) The corresponding calibration plots between the variation of peak current intensity and BPA concentration. (C) The response (△IDPV) of the AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/aptamer electrode upon addition for 100 μM Mg2+, Na+, K+, BPS, L-Gly, and 10 μM BPA. (D) Five parallel assays of BPA.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/aptamer sensor and the electrochemical detection process.
Comparisons of analytical performance of various electrochemical sensors for BPA detection.
Test results of AuNPs/N, S-GQDs aptamer sensor for BPA in tap water.

head.ce:title: The electrochemical behaviors and kinetics of AuNPs/N, S-GQDs composite electrode: A novel label-free amplified BPA aptasensor with extreme sensitivity and selectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu, 610500, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu, 610500, People'’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Process Analysis and Control of Sichuan Universities, Yibin University, Yibin, 644000, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Process Analysis and Control of Sichuan Universities

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Process Analysis and Control of Sichuan Universities, Yibin University, Yibin, 644000, People'’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu, 610500, People's Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (2, 2-bis (4-hydroxyphenyl) propane, BPA), an environmental hormone, is a raw material for the synthesis of polycarbonate plastic and epoxy resins which is commonly used for food-grade packaging materials and plastic products. Adding BPA, the materials become colorless, transparent, durable, lightweight, and more resistant for falling [1]. However, when those materials are used, BPA will migrate into the environment via the food and the mimic estrogen disrupting endocrine, thereby affecting human and animal health even at a low concentration.

Till now, analytical techniques to detect BPA include chromatographic analysis, [2] spectrophotometry [3], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [4]. However, chromatographic analysis is expensive and hard to control, and spectrophotometry is poor accuracy. Furthermore, the preparation cost of the antibody in ELISA is extremely high and it requests a long period. In order to detect the concentration of BPA in the environment, it is particularly important to develop a strategy with simple operation, fast speed and high sensitivity. To solve those issues, electrochemical methods [5], especially electrochemical aptasensors have been utilized as the alternative techniques.

Electrochemical detection has the advantages of simple operation, low price and high accuracy, which has attracted the attention of analysts. With the development of new methods and new technologies, the research and application of various electrochemical sensors have been rapidly promoted, but their performance requirements are also increasing [6]. Due to the interference of other substances, poor stability and complex electron transfer pathway in detection samples, the detection sensitivity is poor, which poses a new challenge to the electrochemical sensor technology. In recent years, Chen et al. [7–9] decorated Au nanoparticle (AuNPs) on glassy carbon electrodes (GCE) to enhance the sensitivity due to the advantages of simple preparation, good biocompatibility and large specific surface area of nanomaterials; Baghayeri et al. [10–12] found that binding BPA aptamer DNA to the surface of gold electrode by sulfhydryl self-assembly could enhance its sensitivity and selectivity; Li et al. [13–16] reported that graphene could be used to enhance the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor. Quantum dots modified GCE can effectively increase the conductivity of electrons. Wang et al. [17–20] discovered that doping graphene quantum dots with N, S, B, S atoms can improve quantum yield, visible light absorption capacity, and electrical properties in varying degrees.

Given that, a novel electrochemical aptasensor platform based on nitrogen and sulfur doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) and AuNPs modified GCE was constructed to detect BPA. N, S-GQDs strengthened the electrochemical response of detection and increased the active area when used for electrode modification. Then AuNPs were dropped on GCE coated with N, S-GQDs. Finally, the anti-BPA aptamer was immobilized on the electrochemical probe by the AuS bond. When BPA exists in solution, it can be specifically identified and captured by the adapter on the electrochemical probe, which makes the channel of the electrochemical probe closed, and other interference ions cannot be detected. This process causes a change in the electrical signal so that BPA analysis can be achieved.

The citric acid (CA), Thiourea, Aptamer, Potassium ferricyanide, Potassium hexacyanoferrate, Sodium dihydrogen phosphate, Disodium hydrogen phosphate, BPA, Bisphenol S (BPS), Chloroauric acid, L-glycine, Tri-(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH), Trisodium citrate, concentrated hydrochloric acid, NaCl, MgCl2, Tris and Potassium chloride were purchased from Chengdu Kelon Co., Ltd. and were all analytically pure. The synthetic anti-BPA aptamer: 5′-SH-(CH2)6-CCG GTG G GT GGT CAG GTG GGA TAG CGT TCC GCG TAT GGC CCA GCG CAT CAC GGG TTC GC A CCA-3′ [21] was obtained from Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

A traditional three-electrode system was employed for all electrochemical experiments, GCE was used as a working electrode, platinum electrode as an auxiliary electrode and calomel electrode as a reference electrode. Electrochemical measurements of cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were performed using an Electrochemical Workstation (CHI 604D, Shanghai, China). Morphology information of the synthesized AuNPs was studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS EVO MA15, Shanghai, China). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used for crystal phase analysis of the prepared N, S-GQDs, and the instrument model used was X Pert PRO MPD (Beijing, China). The characterization of N, S-GQDs was performed using a Fluorescence Spectrometer (Perkin Elmer LS55, American), and Ultraviolet Spectrometer (UV, UV1800, Japan), and an Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR, WQF520, Beijing, China), respectively. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water obtained from a UPC-III-40L Ulupure ultra-pure water purification system (Chengdu, China).

First, 100 mL of a 0.01% aqueous solution of chloroauric acid was placed in a round bottom flask and heated to boiling. Then, 4 mL of a 1% trisodium citrate aqueous solution was accurately added with stirring. Keep heating until the color of the solution turned to red. Then, the solution was preserved in a refrigerator at 4 °C after returning to room temperature [22].

N, S-GQDs were synthesized based on a simple hydrothermal route [23]. In short, 0.84 g CA and 1.24 g thiourea were mixed with 20 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was subsequently transferred into a Teflon lined stainless autoclave, heated to 160 °C in a drying oven for 6 h, and then waited for reactant cooling down to room temperature naturally. The obtained solution was filtered under atmospheric pressure, dialyzed for 24 h and lyophilized to obtain powdery products.

Prior to the modification, the GCE (5 mm) was polished to a mirror-like surface by using Al2O3 powders with a particle size of 1, 0.3 and 0.05 μm. Subsequently, the polished probe was cleaned sequentially by sonication for 5 min in nitric acid (1:1, v/v), ethanol and distilled water to remove impurities on the electrochemical probe surface.

Added 2 mM TCEP to remove the disulfide bonds in the aptamer solution, and then heated it in an oil bath at 90 °C for 10 min. Next, put it in the refrigerator at 4 °C for 15 min. Finally, a denatured anti-BPA aptamer solution was obtained.

Briefly, the GCE surface was polished with Al2O3 and ultrasonically cleaned with ethanol and ultrapure water. 5 μL of 5 g·L−1 N, S-GQDs aqueous solution was dropped onto the treated GCE surface and dried at room temperature. Subsequently, 5 μL of 0.01 wt% AuNPs solution was instilled on the GCE modified with N, S-GQDs. Finally, 5 μL of aptamer and 1 μL of MCH of mixture were added dropwise to the probe, and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 16 h to obtain an aptasensor.

CV was one of the means to characterize aptamer, while EIS was used to characterize different adorned probe. DPV measured the current response to BPA at different concentrations. Those experiments were using [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as a redox molecular probe and operated in a mixing solution of 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− + 0.1 M KCl. CV was carried out in a potential range of - 0.1 to 0.8 V, and EIS in the frequency range of 0.1 to 100,000 Hz.

In this experiment, a novel probe based on the AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/aptamer was constructed to detect BPA (see Scheme. 1 ), which increased the active area and strengthened the electrochemical response [24, 25]. The N, S-GQDs and AuNPs were modified sequentially on the GCE. N, S-GQDs have superior electrical conductivity and can effectively increase the sensitivity of the sensor. AuNPs have high electron density, dielectric properties, catalysis and excellent biocompatibility, which do not affect biological activity. Therefore, the co-modification of N, S-GQDs and AuNPs increases the active area as well as the electrochemical signal of the platform. Since the 5′ end of BPA aptamer is modified with a sulfhydryl group and the outermost layer of the probe is installed with AuNPs, the BPA aptamer can be assembled in the improved sensor through the AuS bond [26].

The detection mechanism of the aptasensor is shown in Fig. 1 . In short, the detection which using aptamer probe to test the concentration of BPA is based on the change in the electronic signal received by the aptsensor. As shown in the upper part of Fig. 1, when BPA is absent in the system, the BPA aptamer immobilized on the probe surface still maintains long-chain extension conformation, and allows electrons in the solution to reach the electrode surface through the electron channel. At this time, a higher electrical signal can be obtained. However, when BPA is presented in the system, the aptamer will specifically recognize BPA, resulting in BPA assembled to the electrode surface, which make the long chain of the BPA aptamer change from a loosely extended conformation to a curled structure, and thus the electron channel on electrode surface is closed (see Fig. 1 lower part). At the same time, the negatively charged phosphoric acid skeleton is exposed on the surface, thereby preventing [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− from reaching the electrode surface. As the concentration of BPA increasing, the response of the electrical signal received by the sensor gradually decreased. This process changes the electrical signal so that different concentrations of BPA can be measured.

Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images magnified 5000 times and transmission electron microscope (TEM) are shown in Fig. 2 . Typical spherical, elliptical and rod-like particles with an average diameter of about 30 nm can be clearly observed by SEM (Fig. 2A) and TEM (Fig. 2B). These particles are evenly distributed on the surface of the conductive glass with major magnification. It can be seen from Fig. 2C that the average particle size distribution of AuNPs is below 100 nm, indicating that the prepared AuNPs can maintain the integrity of the structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Explore the physical and chemical properties of N, S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface functional group of N, S-GQDs characterized by FTIR is shown in Fig. 2D. The FTIR spectrum of N, S-GQDs shows the wide absorption bond at 2500–3000 cm−1, corresponding to stretching vibration of NH and OH. This demonstrate that there are more amino and hydroxyl groups in the N, S-GQDs, and thus N, S-GQDs possess the highly hydrophilic property [27]. The bands at 1705 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1 belong to the vibrational absorption band of CO in COOH and CONH, respectively. While the peaks at 1575 cm−1 and 1405 cm−1 are assigned to the bending vibrations of CC and CN, respectively. Moreover, the bending at 624 cm−1 attributes to the stretching CS [28]. This further prove that N, S-GQDs was synthesized well.

Fig. 2E shows the UV spectrum of N, S-GQDs, the band gap transition centered on 350 nm is caused by the trapping of the excited state energy of the surface state, which makes it produce a strong fluorescence effect [29]. It can be seen from Fig. 2E (a) that the diluted N, S-GQDs aqueous solution is light greenish under the natural light. However, when illuminated with a 365 nm UV lamp, it shows a distinct blue color (Fig. 2E (b)), which further illustrates the fluorescence effect of N, S-GQDs.

The emission fluorescence spectra of N, S-GQDs were shown in Fig. 2F. When the excitation wavelength is at the wavelength of 350 nm, it can clearly observe that the fluorescence emission wavelength is at 440 nm, and as the excitation wavelength being red-shifted, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreasing. This fluorescence behavior associated with the excitation wavelength, which can be attributed to the inconsistent of the surface states, free zigzag sites and different emission defects of N, S-GQDs. The emission wavelength of N, S-GQDs is blue-shifted due to the doped of the N atoms on the surface, which makes an increasing in quantum yield[30].

In order to study phase purity and crystal structure of the synthesized N, S-GQDs, XRD was utilized, and the scanning range was 10–80°. The results obtained are shown in Fig. 2G, there is a broad diffraction peak at 23.2°, which can index to the (002) plan of graphite. The results show that the N, S-GQDs is prepared successfully. It is noteworthy that the main peak shifts to the left after doping N and S compared with graphene, which is the reason that the lattice constant of graphene becomes larger. Besides, it can indicate that the voids and defects of graphene have been enhanced by the doping of N and S elements.

X-ray diffraction imitation was used to obtain the XRD characteristics of N, S-GQDs. The 2-theta scan range parameters were set to 10–80°, the scan step was installed to 0.05, and the X-ray wavelength was set to 1.5. Finally, the XRD simulation diagram shown in the Fig. 3A is obtained. The simulated XRD pattern and the experimentally determined XRD pattern (Fig. 2G) have obvious characteristic peaks near 23°. The emulated material is in good agreement with the actual synthetic material, and the phantom data has a high reliability.

Material studio (MS) was adopted to calculate the density of states (DOS) of N, S-GQDs to reflect the electrical conductivity and bond strength of the synthetic material. It can be seen from Fig. 3B that in the energy range of −10–0 eV, the span of the state density peak is larger, indicating that the bonding effect of N, S-GQDs is stronger. The conduction band is a delocalized energy band in the energy level of a crystalline solid partially filled with electrons. These electrons have high mobility and are responsible for conducting electricity. When the electrons are in the conduction band, they have enough energy to move freely inside the material. This movement generates current [31]. It can be seen from the figure that N, S-GQDs have a very wide conduction band peak, manifesting its good conductivity. More powerfully, it shows that the good affinity between metal ions and heteroatom-doped GQDs is conductive to the electron transfer of quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction kinetic mechanism and parameter calculation
ce:section.ce:para:
CV detection was carried out in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe solution at different scan rates of 10–210 mV·s−1, which was used to studied the effect of scanning rate on the oxidation peak current of electrochemical aptamer sensor. As shown in Fig. 4A, when the scanning rate was 50 mV·s−1, the peak shape of the aptamer probe was the most complete. Therefore, the optimal scanning rate should be set to 50 mV·s−1 when the relevant data were determined by CV. It is noteworthy that the current of the redox peak increases with increasing scan rate, and the current of the redox peak is linear with the square root of the scan rate: I
op = 6.36v 1/2 + 25.03 (R2 = 0.9976) and I
rp = −5.94v 1/2 − 27.45 (R2 = 0.9847) (Fig. 4B). This indicates that the electrochemical reaction occurred with the aptamer sensor is a diffusion control process [32]. The active surface area (A) of the modified electrode can be calculated by the Randles-Sevcik equation [33]:
(1)
I p
= 268600
n
3 / 2
AD
1 / 2
Cv
1 / 2
where I
p is the anodic peak current (A), n is the electron transferred number of the redox event (n = 1), A is the active surface area of the modified electrode, D is diffusion coefficient (D = 6.70 × 10−6 cm2·s−1), C is the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (mol·cm−3), and v is scan rate (V·s−1). It can be calculated that the activation surface area of the electrochemical aptamer probe A = 0.456 cm2, which is 2.3 times of the bare electrode. There is a good linear correlation between the logarithm of the scan rate and the redox potential: E
rp = 0.04 lnv + 0.13 (R2 = 0.9802), as shown in Fig. 4C. Thus, the value of the electron transfer coefficient (α) can be calculated by the following equation [34]:
(2)
E p
=
E 0
+
RT
1 − α
nF
lnv
where E
0 is the formal redox potential, T is the temperature of 298 K, F is Faraday constant, n is the electron-transfer number (n = 1). Thus, α is calculated to be 0.584. Furthermore, utilizing the modified probe and bare GCE for chronoamperometry, results indicated that the square root of time has a linear relationship with the amount of electricity (see Fig. 4D). Fitting equation of aptamer probe is Q
M = 22.48 t
1/2 − 0.62 (R2 = 0.9934). Chronocoulometry was employed to investigate the diffusion coefficient (D) and surface concentration (Г
0 ) of the analyte. With the integrated Cottrell equation [35], the value of D and Г
0 can be calculated, respectively:
(3)
Q = 2 nFAC
Dt
1 / 2
π
− 1 / 2
+
Q ads
+
Q dl
(4)
Q ads
= nFA
Γ 0

Among them, in Eqs. (3) and (4) Q
ads is Faraday oxidation charge (A), Q
dl is electric double layer charge (A), C is analyte concentration (C = 50 μM), A is modified electrode activation area (cm2). The diffusion coefficient of the aptamer sensor can be calculated to D = 8.635 × 10−3 cm−2·s−1, and the surface concentration is Г
0 = 3.53 × 10−10 mol·cm−2.

In addition, the standard rate constant (k) of the aptamer probe is characterized by the electron-transfer kinetics Velasco equation [21]:
(5)
k = 1.11
D
1 2
E p
−
E
p / 2
− 1 / 2
v
1 / 2
where E
p/2 is the half peak potential (mV), v is scan rate (v = 50 mV·s−1), the value of k is calculated to be 9.76 × 10−2 cm·s−1.

[Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was employed as a redox molecular probe in a mixed solution of 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− + 0.1 M KCl. Electrochemical behavior and electrode properties of bare GCE, N, S-GQDs/GCE, AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/GCE, and aptamer probe were detected by CV and EIS.

As shown in Fig. 4E, in the probe solution, CV with a potential range of - 0.1 to 0.8 V was selected to determine the data of each probe. A well-defined pair of oxidation and reduction peaks and peak-to-peak separation are observed from the peak shape. And from the contours of the peaks, oxidation and reduction peaks arise in +0.28 V and +0.18 V respectively, which are attributed to Fe2+ losing electrons in the anode and Fe3+ gaining electrons in the cathode. The impedance is decreased with the increasing of activation area, and a significant raising of peak current is observed after modifying GCE with N, S-GQDs. When AuNPs are bond to N, S-GQDs/GCE, the conduction rate of electrons on the electrode is increasing, thereby accelerating the current response. Finally, when the aptamer is immobilized on AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/GCE surface, the loading of anti-BPA aptamer on the electrode surface will prevent the transfer of electrons on the electrode, as a result, the CV response current is decreased.

EIS for each different sensor has been carried out in the frequency range of 0.1 to 100,000 Hz, as shown in Fig. 4F. The higher frequency semi-circular portion of the EIS spectrum generally represents the electron transfer limiting process, which is equal to electron transfer resistance, while the linear portion of the low frequency corresponded to the diffusion limiting process. When modified GCE with N, S-GQD, the impedance becomes smaller as the activated area of probe being increased. After further modifying AuNPs on N, S-GQDs/GCE, the electron transfer efficiency is improved, resulting in a further reduction of the semicircle diameter. Finally, the electron transfers resistance of AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/GCE increases sharply due to the non-conductivity of anti-BPA aptamer. It obstructes the electron transfer of redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− on the surface of electrode. In addition, the resistance of aptamer modified sensor is greater than that of AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/GCE, which further indicates that the non-conductive adaptor has been successfully attached to AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/GCE through AuS bond. The EIS results are completely consistent with CV, thus further validating the electrode surface modification process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensitivity of AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/aptamer sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/aptamer probe was immersed in the Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 7.0) with different concentrations of BPA in the range of 0.01–100 μM by DPV in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− mixed solution.

As signified in Fig. 5A, the oxidation peak appears near +0.2 V, and the peak current value gradually decreases as the BPA concentration enhancing. This is attributed to the fact that after the aptamer binding to the BPA, its conformation produces a curl, a more negatively charged phosphate backbone is exposed and thus the electron transport channel is reduced, thereby causing exclusion of the redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− near the sensor surface.

The calibration curve of DPV response has shown in Fig. 5B, which shows two linear stages. In the range of 0.1–10 μM, the regression equation is △ I
1 = 17.4488 c
1 + 1.379 (R2 = 0.9574) and △I
2 = 12.2702 c
2 + 5.9903 (R2 = 0.9656). This phenomenon is also observed in the electrochemical detection of BPA [36,37], which can be attributed to the adsorption process of BPA, that is to say, as the concentration of BPA increases, the magnitude of the electrical signal change decreases accordingly, and it has been proved that the coverage of the adsorption makes the concentration of BPA uneven [38,39]. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.03 μM by dividing the three times standard deviations of the five blank measurements by the average value. In addition, we find the performance of N, S-GQDs is comparable or even better than the previously reported BPA electrochemical sensor (Table 1 ), indicating that the prepared aptamer sensor has an application prospect in the analysis of BPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Structural analogues of BPA, including BPS, were used as potentially interference substances to examine the selectivity of AuNPs/N, S-GQDs/aptamer. As demonstrated in Fig. 5C, some ions that are possibly existing in real samples (eg. Mg2+, Na+, K+), BPS, and L-glycine (L-Gly), are tested using the developed method with a concentration 10-fold higher than that of BPA. It can be concluded that the aptasensor prepared in this experiment has the excellent anti-interference ability for selective monitoring BPA.

Blank samples were measured using aptamer probe and five sets of parallel experiments were set up. The results of DPV were displayed in Fig. 5D. It can be seen that the fluctuation amplitude of test current peaks of the five samples is in a high coincidence, and results indicate that the stability of the aptamer sensor is excellent.

The prepared aptamer electrochemical sensor was employed for the determination of BPA in a tap water to demonstrate the potential application capability of the sensor. First, unlabeled tap water would be used as a blank reference for testing, DPV were performed on blank samples and three groups of tap water which contain 10, 5, 1 μM BPA, respectively. The concentration and recovery of BPA in the sample were determined based on the change in current response and the linear equation of sensor. Results were shown in Table 2 , after adding a certain amount of BPA, It can be saw clearly that the concentrations of BPA obtained by the aptamer electrochemical sensor have little difference from the standard addition. Additionally, a good recovery rate (82.6–107%) can be obtained, the relative standard deviation was between 1.98 and 5.54% (n = 3), which proved that the resulting sensor can be applied for highly sensitive detection of BPA in tap water.

Jun Yao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yang Li: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Ming Xie: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Visualization. Qing Yang: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Investigation. Tongyi Liu: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We have reviewed the final version of the manuscript. No conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript, and the manuscript is approved by all authors for publication. I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described is original paper that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51802273), Key Lab of Process Analysis and Control of Sichuan Universities (No. 2019001), Sichuan Science and Technology Program (No. 2019YJ0535), Foundation of Sichuan Educational Committee (No. 14ZB0050), School Technology Fund of Southwest Petroleum University (No. 2013XJZ016), Young scholars' development fund of SWPU (No. 201331010080), College Students' Innovative Entrepreneurial Training Projects in Sichuan Province (No. 201910615001), Key Project of Southwest Petroleum University Open Experiments (No. KSZ19429), Ordinary Project of Southwest Petroleum University Open Experiments (No. KSP19431).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of the environmental fate, effects, and exposures of bisphenol A
Chemosphere

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: CA Staples, PB Dorn, GM Klecka, ST O'Block, LR Harris (1998): A review of the environmental fate, effects, and exposures of bisphenol A. Chemosphere 36:2149.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of 4-nonylphenol and bisphenol A in sewage sludge
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: RJW Meesters, HF Schröder, A Chem (2002): Simultaneous Determination of 4Nonylphenol and Bisphenol A in Sewage Sludge. Analytical Chemistry 74:3566.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrophotometric and high performance liquid chromatographic methods for sensitive determination of bisphenol A
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
